兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kabutozuka Kofun | image =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] | Caption = July 2005 | location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14 | Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} | Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} | Burial facility = | Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long | Buried individual = | Excavated artifacts = | Historic site designation = | Notes = }} '''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.
The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.

Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

revision.text:
兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kabutozuka Kofun | image =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] | Caption = July 2005 | location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14 | Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} | Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} | Burial facility = | Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long | Buried individual = | Excavated artifacts = | Historic site designation = | Notes = }} '''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.
The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kabutozuka Kofun | image =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] | Caption = July 2005 | location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14 | Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} | Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} | Burial facility = | Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long | Buried individual = | Excavated artifacts = | Historic site designation = | Notes = }} '''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.
The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

[[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kabutozuka Kofun | image =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] | Caption = July 2005 | location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14 | Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} | Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} | Burial facility = | Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long | Buried individual = | Excavated artifacts = | Historic site designation = | Notes = }} '''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kabutozuka Kofun | image =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] | Caption = July 2005 | location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14 | Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} | Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} | Burial facility = | Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long | Buried individual = | Excavated artifacts = | Historic site designation = | Notes = }} '''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.
The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.
The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kabutozuka Kofun | image =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] | Caption = July 2005 | location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14 | Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} | Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} | Burial facility = | Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long | Buried individual = | Excavated artifacts = | Historic site designation = | Notes = }} '''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.
The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.
The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.
However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.
In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.
In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979. * Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976. * Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]

Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.== External Links ==* [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]]]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education)** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kofun in Miyagi Prefecture]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:History of Taihaku Ward]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Scallop-shaped keyhole tumuli]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:01-17]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:1 revision imported]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:bump 1-20]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]{{Kofun navbar}}]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[ja:兜塚古墳]]

revision.text: 兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-qq]] ]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:mass bump]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-qq]] ]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:01-17]]]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]]{{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =]][[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]]| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.== location and Structure ==Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status ==The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.== Excavated Artifacts ==Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.== References =={{reflist}}== Further Reading ==* Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.== External Links ==* [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]]]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education)** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kofun in Miyagi Prefecture]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:History of Taihaku Ward]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Scallop-shaped keyhole tumuli]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-qq]] ]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:01-17]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:1 revision imported]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:bump 1-20]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]{{Kofun navbar}}]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] ]][[ja:兜塚古墳]]

The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status ==The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.== Excavated Artifacts ==Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.== References =={{reflist}}== Further Reading ==* Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.== External Links ==* [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category:kk]]]][[Category:/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education)** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html Kabutozuka Kofun]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kofun in Miyagi Prefecture]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:History of Taihaku Ward]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Scallop-shaped keyhole tumuli]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-qq]] ]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:01-17]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:1 revision imported]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:bump 1-20]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]{{Kofun navbar}}]][[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] ]][[ja:兜塚古墳]] [[Category:new pages]]

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-qq]] ]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:mass bump]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-qq]] ]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:dd]]]][[Category:kk]]]][[Category:01-17]]]][[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]]{{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =]][[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]]| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.== location and Structure ==Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status ==The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.== Excavated Artifacts ==Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.== References =={{reflist}}== Further Reading ==* Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931.
