兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *[[Category:nowiki former closing]] Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Burial Mound Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *[[Category:nowiki former closing]] Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Burial Mound Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *[[Category:nowiki former closing]] Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *[[Category:nowiki former closing]] Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *[[Category:nowiki former closing]] Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.

Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. {{日本の古墳 |名称 = 兜塚古墳 |画像 =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション =2005年7月 |所在地 = [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[太白区]]根岸町14 | 緯度度 = 38 | 緯度分 = 14 | 緯度秒 = 19.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 140 |経度分 = 52 | 経度秒 = 39.0 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |形状 = [[帆立貝形古墳]]か |築造年代 =[[5世紀]]後半 |埋葬施設 = |規模 =全長約75[[メートル]] |被葬者 = |出土品 = |史跡指定 = |特記事項 = |地図2={{Location map|Japan Miyagi Prefecture|relief=1|lat_deg=38|lat_min=14|lat_sec=19.2|lon_deg=140|lon_min=52|lon_sec=39.0|label=兜塚古墳|mark=帆立貝形古墳-南東.svg|marksize=30|caption=所在地|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|border=none}} }} '''兜塚古墳'''（かぶとづかこふん）は、現在の[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[太白区]]、[[宮城県仙台南高等学校]]の校地にある[[古墳]]である。[[帆立貝形古墳]]で、築造は[[5世紀]]後半と推定されている。全長約75[[メートル]]で、[[葺石]]と[[埴輪]]を伴い、同時期の[[仙台平野]]の古墳の中では大きなものであった。
== 立地と構造 == [[仙台平野]]を流れる[[広瀬川 (宮城県)|広瀬川]]の南岸（右岸）、[[大年寺山]]東麓の[[沖積平野]]に築かれた。似たような立地で兜塚よりやや小ぶりの古墳が[[一塚古墳]]、[[二塚古墳 (仙台市)|二塚古墳]]、[[裏町古墳]]などである。やはり近辺の[[大野田古墳群]]は、それらよりさらに一回り小さく、密集した古墳群である。兜塚古墳などの被葬者は大野田古墳群の被葬者の上に立つ首長層と説かれる<ref>『仙台市史』特別編（考古資料）「大野田古墳群」260頁。</ref>。
現状は径約50メートルの[[円墳]]のように見えるが、これは市道兜塚線<ref>仙台市道太白385号・兜塚線（最小幅員7.80m、最大幅員12.00m、延長483.2m）</ref>によって前方部が切り取られたためである。推定規模は後円部の径が62.4メートル、前方部の長さ15メートルで主軸長約75メートル、前方部前端の幅が約30メートル、後円部の高さ6.8メートル。周溝の幅は後円部で13メートル<ref name="#1">『仙台市史』特別編（考古資料）「兜塚古墳」256頁。</ref>。前方部が著しく小さな帆立貝形である。
後円部は2段が肉眼で明瞭にみてとれ、3段以上ありそうにも見える。この段を兜に見立てたのが名の由来であろう。段の斜面には[[葺石]]が置かれた。また、[[円筒埴輪]]と[[朝顔形埴輪]]が並べられた。
== 伝説・発掘・現況 == 兜塚の名は、兜を伏せたような段々の形からついたと思われる。地元では、[[慶長]]6年（[[1601年]]）に近所で起きた[[小人騒動]]の死者を埋めたため'''小人塚'''（こびとづか）と呼ぶという説が[[17世紀]]のうちに生まれた<ref>清水東四郎『宮城県通史』5頁。</ref>。[[明治]]時代までの仙台の人々にはこの伝説で知られた塚で、古い地図には小人塚やコビト塚と書いたものもある<ref>琴田鶽太郎・東洋造画館の[[大正]]元年（[[1912年]]）「最新版仙台市全図」（『100年前の仙台を歩く 仙台地図散歩』今野印刷所、2009年に復刻版収録）に甲塚又小人塚とあり、カブト、コビトとルビを振る。盛文館・木村文助の[[昭和]]11年（[[1926年]]）改正版「最新刊地番入仙台市地図中央部」と昭和18年（[[1933年]]）「仙台市中央部地図」（塔文社レトロマップシリーズ2『昭和11年、18年、27年の仙台と現在の仙台』に復刻版収録）は兜塚とする。</ref>。
しかし、明治時代の考古学者の目には既に古墳であることが明らかであった。問題はこれが[[円墳]]なのか前方後円墳の前方部が壊されたものなのかで、説が分かれていた<ref>清水東四郎『宮城県通史』5頁は「丸塚」、つまり円墳とする。</ref>。
[[1959年]]（[[昭和]]34年）、現在の宮城県仙台南高等学校の校地などに広がっていた宮城県農学校<ref>当時の[[名取郡]][[茂ヶ崎村]]、現在の仙台市[[太白区]]にあった。後に[[宮城県農業高等学校]]となり、[[名取市]]に移転。</ref>に勤務していた沼倉吉兵衛（[[仙台白菜]]の生みの親）の胸像が、農学校の同窓会によって古墳上に設置された。
[[1977年]]（昭和52年）に宮城県教員委員会が周囲の東側の発掘調査を実施し、馬蹄形の溝を発見したため、前方部が小さい帆立貝式の前方後円墳だろうということになった。続いて[[1988年]]（昭和63年）に南側も発掘されたが、主体部に調査は及んでいない。
== 出土物 == 周辺調査のみということもあり、葺石に使われた丸い河原石多数を除けば、円筒埴輪と朝顔形埴輪の破片がわずかに見つかっているだけである。埴輪片は近くの[[富沢窯跡]]で出土したものと似ている<ref name="#1"/>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 伊東信雄「仙台市の古墳」、『仙台郷土史の研究』、宝文堂、1979年。 * 清水東四郎『宮城県通史』、新約社書店、1931年。覆刻版は宝文堂により1976年発行。 * 仙台市史編さん委員会『仙台市史』特別編2（考古資料）、仙台市、1995年。「兜塚古墳」「大野田古墳群」ともに執筆は藤沢敦。
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳] {{考古学}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かふとつかこふん}} [[Category:宮城県の古墳]] [[Category:太白区の歴史]] [[Category:帆立貝形古墳]]

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *[[Category:nowiki former closing]] Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.

Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. 兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)--> [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:01-17]] [[ja:兜塚古墳 (仙台市)]] {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| Caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| Latitude degrees = 38 | Latitude minutes = 14 | Latitude seconds = 19.2 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 140 | Longitude minutes = 53 | Longitude seconds = 3.2 | E/W = E| Map country code = JP| Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| Construction era = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| Burial facility =| Scale = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| Excavated artifacts =| Historic site designation =| Notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and Structure == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in scale, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original dimensions are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.

{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 兜塚古墳 |画像 =[[File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg|300px]] |画像キャプション =2005年7月 |所在地 = [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[太白区]]根岸町14 | 緯度度 = 38 | 緯度分 = 14 | 緯度秒 = 19.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 140 |経度分 = 52 | 経度秒 = 39.0 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |形状 = [[帆立貝形古墳]]か |築造年代 =[[5世紀]]後半 |埋葬施設 = |規模 =全長約75[[メートル]] |被葬者 = |出土品 = |史跡指定 = |特記事項 = |地図2={{Location map|Japan Miyagi Prefecture|relief=1|lat_deg=38|lat_min=14|lat_sec=19.2|lon_deg=140|lon_min=52|lon_sec=39.0|label=兜塚古墳|mark=帆立貝形古墳-南東.svg|marksize=30|caption=所在地|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|border=none}} }} '''兜塚古墳'''（かぶとづかこふん）は、現在の[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[太白区]]、[[宮城県仙台南高等学校]]の校地にある[[古墳]]である。[[帆立貝形古墳]]で、築造は[[5世紀]]後半と推定されている。全長約75[[メートル]]で、[[葺石]]と[[埴輪]]を伴い、同時期の[[仙台平野]]の古墳の中では大きなものであった。
== 立地と構造 == [[仙台平野]]を流れる[[広瀬川 (宮城県)|広瀬川]]の南岸（右岸）、[[大年寺山]]東麓の[[沖積平野]]に築かれた。似たような立地で兜塚よりやや小ぶりの古墳が[[一塚古墳]]、[[二塚古墳 (仙台市)|二塚古墳]]、[[裏町古墳]]などである。やはり近辺の[[大野田古墳群]]は、それらよりさらに一回り小さく、密集した古墳群である。兜塚古墳などの被葬者は大野田古墳群の被葬者の上に立つ首長層と説かれる<ref>『仙台市史』特別編（考古資料）「大野田古墳群」260頁。</ref>。
現状は径約50メートルの[[円墳]]のように見えるが、これは市道兜塚線<ref>仙台市道太白385号・兜塚線（最小幅員7.80m、最大幅員12.00m、延長483.2m）</ref>によって前方部が切り取られたためである。推定規模は後円部の径が62.4メートル、前方部の長さ15メートルで主軸長約75メートル、前方部前端の幅が約30メートル、後円部の高さ6.8メートル。周溝の幅は後円部で13メートル<ref name="#1">『仙台市史』特別編（考古資料）「兜塚古墳」256頁。</ref>。前方部が著しく小さな帆立貝形である。
後円部は2段が肉眼で明瞭にみてとれ、3段以上ありそうにも見える。この段を兜に見立てたのが名の由来であろう。段の斜面には[[葺石]]が置かれた。また、[[円筒埴輪]]と[[朝顔形埴輪]]が並べられた。
== 伝説・発掘・現況 == 兜塚の名は、兜を伏せたような段々の形からついたと思われる。地元では[[1601年]]（[[慶長]]6年）に近所で起きた[[小人騒動]]の死者を埋めたため'''小人塚'''（こびとづか）と呼ぶという説が[[17世紀]]のうちに生まれた<ref>清水東四郎『宮城県通史』5頁。</ref>。[[明治時代]]までの仙台の人々にはこの伝説で知られた塚で、古い地図には小人塚やコビト塚と書いたものもある<ref>琴田鶽太郎・東洋造画館の[[1912年]]（[[大正]]元年）「最新版仙台市全図」（『100年前の仙台を歩く 仙台地図散歩』今野印刷所、2009年に復刻版収録）に甲塚又小人塚とあり、カブト、コビトとルビを振る。盛文館・木村文助の[[1926年]]（[[昭和]]11年）改正版「最新刊地番入仙台市地図中央部」と[[1933年]]（昭和18年）「仙台市中央部地図」（塔文社レトロマップシリーズ2『昭和11年、18年、27年の仙台と現在の仙台』に復刻版収録）は兜塚とする。</ref>。
しかし、明治時代の考古学者の目には既に古墳であることが明らかであった。問題はこれが[[円墳]]なのか前方後円墳の前方部が壊されたものなのかで、意見が分かれていた<ref>清水東四郎『宮城県通史』5頁は「丸塚」、つまり円墳とする。</ref>。
[[1959年]]（[[昭和]]34年）、現在の宮城県仙台南高等学校の校地などに広がっていた宮城県農学校<ref>当時の[[名取郡]][[茂ヶ崎村]]、現在の仙台市[[太白区]]にあった。後に[[宮城県農業高等学校]]となり、[[名取市]]に移転。</ref>に勤務していた沼倉吉兵衛（[[仙台白菜]]の生みの親）の胸像が、農学校の同窓会によって古墳上に設置された。
[[1977年]]（昭和52年）に宮城県教員委員会が周囲の東側の[[発掘調査]]を実施し、周溝が馬蹄形を呈する部分を検出したため、前方部が小さい[[帆立貝形古墳]]だろうと推定された{{Sfn|宮城県教育委員会|1978|pp=200-212}}。続いて[[1988年]]（昭和63年）に南側も発掘されたが、主体部に調査は及んでいない。
== 出土物 == 周辺調査のみということもあり、葺石に使われた丸い河原石多数を除けば、円筒埴輪と朝顔形埴輪の破片がわずかに見つかっているだけである。埴輪片は近くの[[富沢窯跡]]で出土したものと似ている<ref name="#1"/>。

The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>Maps such as the 1912 "Latest Map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the former Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the former {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== Excavated Artifacts == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few artifacts have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
