Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} 兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} 兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.

<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} 兜塚古墳 (仙台市)
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000036.html 兜塚古墳]

<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
<!--Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)-->
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Scallop Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| name = Kabutozuka Kofun| image =File:Kabutozuka-kofun 2005-07.jpg| caption = July 2005| location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Negishimachi 14| coordinates = 38 | coordinates = 14 | coordinates = 19.2 | coordinates = N | coordinates = 140 | coordinates = 53 | coordinates = 3.2 | coordinates = E| map = JP| type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| built = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}| burial =| map_size = Approx. 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} long| Buried individual =| excavations =| Historic Site Historic Site =| notes =}}'''Kabutozuka Kofun''' (兜塚古墳) is a {{ill|tumulus|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located on the grounds of the present-day {{ill|Miyagi Prefectural Sendai Minami High School|ja|宮城県仙台南高等学校|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is a scallop-shaped {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} estimated to have been constructed in the late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Measuring approximately 75 {{ill|meters|ja|メートル|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in total length, it features {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the largest tumuli in the Sendai Plain during its era.
== location and burial == Kabutozuka Kofun is situated on the southern (right) bank of the {{ill|Hirose River (Miyagi)|ja|広瀬川 (宮城県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the alluvial plain at the eastern foot of {{ill|Ōtoshiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Several smaller tumuli, such as {{ill|Hitotsuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|二塚古墳 (仙台市)|1=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, are located in similar settings. The nearby {{ill|Ōnodera Kofun Group|ja|大野田古墳群|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, smaller in map_size, consists of a denser cluster of tumuli. The interred individuals in Kabutozuka Kofun and similar sites are thought to belong to a higher-ranking leadership class compared to those buried in the Ōnodera group<ref>‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Ōnodera Kofun Group," p. 260.</ref>.The tumulus currently appears as a circular mound approximately 50 meters in diameter due to the truncation of the front section by the Kabutozuka Line municipal road<ref>Sendai Municipal Road Taihaku 385 (Kabutozuka Line), minimum width 7.80 m, maximum width 12.00 m, length 483.2 m.</ref>. Its original area are estimated as follows: a posterior circular section 62.4 meters in diameter, a 15-meter-long anterior section, an overall axial length of approximately 75 meters, a width of 30 meters at the front end of the anterior section, and a height of 6.8 meters in the posterior section. The surrounding moat is about 13 meters wide near the posterior section<ref name="#1">‘Sendai City History’, Special Edition (Archaeological Materials), "Kabutozuka Kofun," p. 256.</ref>. The tumulus is of a scallop-shaped design with a notably small anterior section.The posterior section appears to have two tiers clearly visible to the naked eye, with the possibility of three or more tiers. The mound's stepped appearance is thought to have inspired its name, likened to an inverted kabuto (helmet). The slopes of these tiers were covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} were arranged along the tiers.
== Legends, Excavations, and Current Status == The name Kabutozuka likely originates from its stepped appearance resembling an inverted kabuto (helmet). Locally, it was also known as "Kobitozuka" (小人塚, "Mound of the Little People"), stemming from a legend that it was used to bury victims of a "little people disturbance" in the nearby area during the {{ill|Keichō era|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1601). This tale was widespread among the residents of Sendai until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5.</ref>. Older maps of Sendai sometimes map_dot_label the mound as Kobitozuka<ref>maps such as the 1912 "Latest map of Sendai City" by Kotoda Gakutarō and Toyo Zogakakukan, and the 1926 and 1933 revisions by Moribunkan and Kimura Bunzuke also map_dot_label it as Kobitozuka.</ref>.However, by the Meiji period, archaeologists recognized it as a tumulus. Debate centered on whether it was originally a circular tumulus or a truncated keyhole-shaped tumulus<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ p. 5, describes it as "Maruzuka" (circular tumulus).</ref>.In 1959 (Shōwa 34), a bust of Numakura Kichibei, the creator of {{ill|Sendai Hakusai|ja|仙台白菜|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, was erected on the tumulus by the alumni association of the typeer Miyagi Agricultural School, which occupied part of the current school grounds<ref>Previously located in the typeer {{ill|Natori District, Miyagi|ja|名取郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Moshigasaki Village, now in Sendai's Taihaku Ward. Later relocated to {{ill|Natori|ja|名取市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural High School.</ref>.In 1977 (Shōwa 52), the Miyagi Prefectural Board of Education conducted excavations on the eastern side of the mound, uncovering a horseshoe-shaped moat, suggesting it to be a scallop-shaped keyhole tumulus. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), further excavations were conducted on the southern side, although the central burial chamber remains uninvestigated.
== burial goods == Due to the limited scope of the surrounding investigations, few burial goods have been discovered. Aside from numerous rounded river stones used as {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of cylindrical and morning glory-shaped {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} have been found. These fragments resemble those excavated at the nearby {{ill|Tomizawa Kiln Site|ja|富沢窯跡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Further Reading == *Nao Itō, "Tombs of Sendai City," in ‘Studies of Sendai Local History,’ Hobundo, 1979.* Tōshirō Shimizu, ‘Miyagi Prefecture General History,’ Shinyakusha Bookstore, 1931. Reprinted by Hobundo in 1976.* Sendai City History Compilation Committee, ‘Sendai City History,’ Special Edition 2 (Archaeological Materials), Sendai City, 1995. Articles on "Kabutozuka Kofun" and "Ōnodera Kofun Group" authored by Atsushi Fujisawa.
